text:
COMPREHENSIVE SOLID TUMOR NEXT-GENERATION SEQUENCING REPORT

========================================================
INSTITUTIONAL MOLECULAR PATHOLOGY LABORATORY
CAP/CLIA ACCREDITED
========================================================

PATIENT INFORMATION
Patient Name: Margaret Osei-Bonsu
Date of Birth: [on file]
Sex: Female
Medical Record Number: [on file]
Ordering Physician: [Oncology]
Diagnosis (Submitting): Metastatic malignant melanoma, BRAF V600E-positive, Stage IV (M1c)

SPECIMEN INFORMATION
Specimen ID: NGS-2024-MELX-0412
Specimen Type: Formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded (FFPE) tissue block
Anatomic Site: Left axillary lymph node (excision)
Date of Procedure: [on file]
Date Received in Laboratory: [on file]
Date Report Issued: [on file]
Tumor Content (Estimated by Pathology Review): 65% (verified by H&E review prior to extraction)
DNA Input: 200 ng (sufficient for analysis)
RNA Input: 100 ng (sufficient for analysis)

ASSAY INFORMATION
Assay Platform: Comprehensive Solid Tumor Panel (500-gene; DNA + RNA hybrid capture)
Sequencing Platform: Illumina NovaSeq 6000
Mean Coverage (DNA): 720x (target: ≥500x)
Mean Coverage (RNA): High quality; gene expression and fusion analysis performed
Variant Allele Frequency (VAF) Reporting Threshold: ≥5%
Analysis: Single nucleotide variants (SNVs), insertions/deletions (indels), copy number alterations (CNAs), gene fusions/rearrangements, tumor mutational burden (TMB), microsatellite instability (MSI)
Quality Control: All QC metrics passed

========================================================
CLINICAL SUMMARY AND INDICATION
========================================================

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 58-year-old female with a history of dysplastic nevus syndrome and newly diagnosed Stage IV (AJCC 8th edition, M1c) cutaneous malignant melanoma arising from the skin of the trunk. At presentation, she has bulky bilateral axillary lymphadenopathy (left axilla 4.8 cm with central necrosis), multiple hepatic metastases (largest 3.2 cm, segment VI), and subcutaneous satellite nodules along the left flank. Serum LDH is elevated at 2.4x the upper limit of normal. Brain MRI at staging is negative for intracranial metastases. BRAF V600E mutation was previously identified by immunohistochemistry (VE1 antibody, strongly positive 3+) on the left axillary lymph node excision specimen. PD-L1 by 22C3 pharmDx is TPS 30%, CPS 55. Next-generation sequencing was ordered to provide a comprehensive molecular profile to guide systemic therapy selection and identify any additional clinically actionable alterations.

========================================================
RESULTS SUMMARY
========================================================

CLINICALLY ACTIONABLE / TIER I AND TIER II ALTERATIONS

1. BRAF p.V600E (c.1799T>A) — Pathogenic (Tier I)
   Gene/Locus: BRAF, chromosome 7q34
   Alteration Type: Missense mutation, single nucleotide variant
   Variant Allele Frequency: 48%
   Exon: 15
   Classification: Pathogenic; well-established oncogenic driver in cutaneous melanoma
   Clinical Significance: BRAF p.V600E is the most common activating BRAF mutation in cutaneous melanoma (~50% of cases), resulting in constitutive activation of the MAPK/ERK signaling pathway. FDA-approved combination targeted therapies available: dabrafenib (Tafinlar) + trametinib (Mekinist), vemurafenib (Zelboraf) + cobimetinib (Cotellic), and encorafenib (Braftovi) + binimetinib (Mektovi). This mutation was confirmed concordant with prior IHC result (VE1 antibody 3+).
   Relevant Evidence: Level A (FDA-approved therapy available for this alteration in this tumor type).

2. CDKN2A — Homozygous Deletion — Pathogenic (Tier I)
   Gene/Locus: CDKN2A (p16/INK4A and p14/ARF), chromosome 9p21.3
   Alteration Type: Copy number alteration — homozygous deletion (0 copies)
   Classification: Pathogenic; loss of tumor suppressor
   Clinical Significance: CDKN2A encodes both p16/INK4A (a CDK4/6 inhibitor that suppresses cell cycle entry via Rb pathway) and p14/ARF (a positive regulator of p53 via MDM2). Homozygous deletion results in complete loss of both tumor suppressive functions. CDKN2A deletion is among the most common somatic alterations in cutaneous melanoma (~50% of cases). Loss of p16 cooperates with BRAF V600E in driving unchecked cell cycle progression. Co-deletion of CDKN2B (p15/INK4B) noted (see below).
   Relevant Evidence: Tier I prognostic alteration; no direct FDA-approved targeted therapy, but relevant in context of CDK4/6 inhibitor clinical trials.

3. NF1 p.Q1300* (c.3898C>T) — Pathogenic (Tier I)
   Gene/Locus: NF1 (neurofibromin 1), chromosome 17q11.2
   Alteration Type: Nonsense mutation (premature stop codon), single nucleotide variant
   Variant Allele Frequency: 52%
   Exon: 28
   Classification: Pathogenic; loss-of-function mutation
   Clinical Significance: NF1 encodes neurofibromin, a GTPase-activating protein (GAP) that negatively regulates RAS by promoting GTP hydrolysis. Loss-of-function NF1 mutations result in constitutive RAS-GTP (active) accumulation, leading to activation of downstream MAPK/ERK and PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathways. NF1-mutant melanomas comprise the "NF1" molecular subtype (~15% of cutaneous melanomas). The co-occurrence of BRAF V600E and NF1 loss is less common but has been described; this combination may paradoxically reduce MEK pathway dependence through RAS bypass signaling, a potential mechanism of acquired resistance to BRAF/MEK inhibition. High VAF (52%) is consistent with biallelic inactivation or loss of heterozygosity at this locus.
   Relevant Evidence: Tier I; associated with RAS pathway activation. Relevant to MEK inhibitor and BRAF inhibitor sensitivity. May predispose to earlier acquired resistance to BRAF-targeted therapy.

========================================================
ADDITIONAL PATHOGENIC / LIKELY PATHOGENIC VARIANTS
========================================================

4. TP53 p.R248W (c.742C>T) — Pathogenic (Tier II)
   Gene/Locus: TP53, chromosome 17p13.1
   Alteration Type: Missense mutation (gain-of-function/dominant negative)
   Variant Allele Frequency: 41%
   Exon: 7
   Classification: Pathogenic
   Clinical Significance: TP53 p.R248W is one of the most common and well-characterized hotspot mutations in human cancer. This missense mutation at a DNA-contact residue results in loss of normal p53 tumor suppressor function and confers oncogenic gain-of-function properties (transcriptional activation of pro-oncogenic targets, promotion of invasion and genomic instability). TP53 mutation is present in approximately 19% of cutaneous melanomas and is associated with more aggressive disease biology.

5. PTEN p.L108R (c.323T>G) — Likely Pathogenic (Tier II)
   Gene/Locus: PTEN, chromosome 10q23.31
   Alteration Type: Missense mutation
   Variant Allele Frequency: 22%
   Exon: 4
   Classification: Likely pathogenic
   Clinical Significance: PTEN is a tumor suppressor phosphatase that negatively regulates the PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling pathway. Loss-of-function mutations in PTEN are found in approximately 10–20% of cutaneous melanomas and result in constitutive AKT activation. The p.L108R substitution affects the phosphatase domain and is predicted to impair catalytic activity. PTEN loss in the context of BRAF V600E may contribute to acquired resistance to BRAF-targeted therapy by activating the PI3K pathway as an escape route. VAF of 22% may indicate subclonal presence or heterozygous mutation with preserved second allele.

6. RAC1 p.P29S (c.85C>T) — Likely Pathogenic (Tier II)
   Gene/Locus: RAC1, chromosome 7p22.1
   Alteration Type: Missense mutation (activating)
   Variant Allele Frequency: 18%
   Exon: 2
   Classification: Likely pathogenic
   Clinical Significance: RAC1 p.P29S is a recurrent UV-signature hotspot mutation found in approximately 5–9% of cutaneous melanomas and is the most common RAC1 alteration in this tumor type. The mutation constitutively activates RAC1 GTPase, which drives cell cycle entry, cytoskeletal remodeling, and activation of PAK signaling. RAC1 p.P29S has been associated with primary resistance to BRAF inhibitors through MAPK-independent mechanisms. The subclonal VAF (18%) may reflect intratumoral heterogeneity. The C>T transition at a dipyrimidine context is consistent with UV-induced mutagenesis.

7. ARID2 p.E1242fs (c.3724_3725del) — Likely Pathogenic (Tier II)
   Gene/Locus: ARID2, chromosome 12q12
   Alteration Type: Frameshift deletion (2 bp), loss-of-function
   Variant Allele Frequency: 35%
   Exon: 18
   Classification: Likely pathogenic
   Clinical Significance: ARID2 encodes a subunit of the PBAF (SWI/SNF) chromatin remodeling complex. Loss-of-function mutations in ARID2 are found in approximately 7–13% of cutaneous melanomas and result in impaired chromatin remodeling, altered gene expression, and dysregulation of the interferon-gamma signaling pathway. ARID2 loss has been associated with altered tumor immune microenvironment and may influence responsiveness to immune checkpoint inhibitors.

8. TERT Promoter c.-124C>T (C228T) — Pathogenic (Tier II)
   Gene/Locus: TERT promoter, chromosome 5p15.33
   Alteration Type: Promoter mutation (non-coding SNV, creates novel ETS transcription factor binding site)
   Variant Allele Frequency: 55%
   Classification: Pathogenic
   Clinical Significance: The TERT promoter C228T mutation is one of two recurrent hotspot mutations (C228T and C250T) found in approximately 70–85% of cutaneous melanomas and represents a hallmark genomic alteration of this tumor type. This mutation creates a de novo ETS family transcription factor binding site, leading to marked upregulation of telomerase reverse transcriptase (TERT) expression, telomere maintenance, and cellular immortalization. High VAF (55%) reflects clonal prevalence. The TERT promoter mutation is a strongly UV-signature-associated change consistent with solar radiation-induced carcinogenesis.

========================================================
COPY NUMBER ALTERATIONS
========================================================

9. MAP2K1 (MEK1) — Copy Number Gain (Amplification)
   Gene/Locus: MAP2K1, chromosome 15q22.31
   Alteration Type: Focal copy number gain
   Estimated Copy Number: 5 copies (relative to diploid baseline of 2)
   Classification: Variant of potential clinical significance
   Clinical Significance: MAP2K1 encodes MEK1, a central kinase in the MAPK/ERK signaling cascade immediately downstream of both BRAF and RAS. Copy number gain or amplification of MAP2K1 is an emerging mechanism of acquired resistance to BRAF inhibitors and BRAF/MEK inhibitor combinations in melanoma, providing a gene dosage-dependent increase in MEK signaling output that may partially bypass BRAF inhibition. In combination with the co-occurring NF1 loss and PTEN mutation, this finding raises concern for potential intrinsic or acquired resistance mechanisms that may limit the durability of BRAF/MEK-targeted therapy.

10. CDKN2A/CDKN2B — Homozygous Deletion (co-deletion)
    Gene/Locus: CDKN2A and CDKN2B, chromosome 9p21.3
    Alteration Type: Contiguous homozygous deletion involving both CDKN2A and CDKN2B loci
    Estimated Copy Number: 0 copies
    Classification: Pathogenic
    Clinical Significance: Co-deletion of CDKN2A (p16/INK4A, p14/ARF) and CDKN2B (p15/INK4B) represents complete inactivation of the INK4 locus, eliminating multiple tumor suppressive barriers to cell cycle progression. This is one of the most common focal deletions in melanoma.

11. MYC — Copy Number Gain
    Gene/Locus: MYC, chromosome 8q24.21
    Alteration Type: Copy number gain (low-level)
    Estimated Copy Number: 3 copies
    Classification: Variant of uncertain significance / potential oncogenic relevance
    Clinical Significance: MYC gain is observed in a subset of melanomas and other solid tumors. Low-level gain (3 copies) may reflect aneuploidy or early amplification. MYC drives transcriptional programs associated with cell proliferation, metabolic reprogramming, and immune evasion. Not directly targetable with approved agents.

12. CCND1 — Copy Number Gain
    Gene/Locus: CCND1 (Cyclin D1), chromosome 11q13.3
    Alteration Type: Copy number gain (low-level)
    Estimated Copy Number: 3 copies
    Classification: Variant of uncertain significance
    Clinical Significance: CCND1 encodes Cyclin D1, which forms a complex with CDK4/6 to drive G1/S cell cycle transition. Low-level gain may contribute to cell cycle dysregulation, particularly in the context of CDKN2A/CDKN2B deletion (which eliminates the endogenous CDK4/6 inhibitors p16 and p15). CDK4/6 inhibitors (e.g., palbociclib, ribociclib, abemaciclib) are not currently approved for melanoma but are being investigated in clinical trials, particularly in Rb-pathway-altered tumors.

========================================================
NEGATIVE / ABSENT FINDINGS (CLINICALLY RELEVANT)
========================================================

- NRAS: No mutation detected (codons 12, 13, 61, 146; exons 2 and 3 fully covered)
  Note: Mutually exclusive NRAS mutations (Q61R/K/L, G12D, G13R) are absent, consistent with BRAF V600E-driven disease.

- KIT: No mutation detected (exons 9, 11, 13, 17)
  Note: KIT mutations/amplifications are more common in acral lentiginous and mucosal melanomas; absence is consistent with cutaneous primary.

- GNA11 / GNAQ: No mutation detected
  Note: Mutations in these genes are characteristic of uveal melanoma; their absence further supports cutaneous origin.

- IDH1 / IDH2: No mutation detected

- Gene Fusions / Rearrangements: No oncogenic fusions detected by RNA analysis (including ALK, RET, MET, ROS1, NTRK1/2/3, NRG1, FGFR1-4). No BRAF fusions detected.

- HRAS: No mutation detected

- PIK3CA: No mutation detected

========================================================
BIOMARKER SUMMARY
========================================================

Microsatellite Instability (MSI) Status:
  Result: MICROSATELLITE STABLE (MSS)
  Method: Fragment analysis of 5 mononucleotide repeat markers (BAT-25, BAT-26, MONO-27, NR-21, NR-24)
  Interpretation: No MSI-H detected. MSI-H is uncommon in cutaneous melanoma (<5% of cases). MSS status is consistent with expected biology for this tumor type.

Tumor Mutational Burden (TMB):
  Result: 42 mutations/Mb — HIGH
  Method: Calculated from somatic nonsynonymous SNVs and indels across the coding regions of the 500-gene panel; germline variants subtracted using validated filtering algorithm.
  Interpretation: TMB of 42 mut/Mb is markedly elevated and places this tumor in the high TMB category (threshold: ≥10 mut/Mb per FDA pembrolizumab tumor-agnostic approval). This is consistent with the UV-driven mutational landscape of cutaneous melanoma, where median TMB is typically 10–20 mut/Mb; the exceptionally high TMB in this case may reflect extensive UV damage accumulated over decades in the context of dysplastic nevus syndrome. High TMB is associated with increased neoantigen burden and, in many tumor types, improved response to immune checkpoint inhibition. Note: this assay-based TMB result is consistent with the overall high mutational complexity observed and is reinforced by PD-L1 TPS 30% / CPS 55 by IHC.

Mutational Signature Analysis:
  Dominant Signature: UV-induced mutagenesis (COSMIC Signature SBS7a/SBS7b)
  Interpretation: The predominant mutational signature is consistent with ultraviolet radiation-induced C>T transitions at dipyrimidine contexts (CC>TT dinucleotide substitutions), the hallmark mutational pattern of solar radiation-driven skin cancers. This finding is strongly concordant with the clinical diagnosis of cutaneous melanoma arising on sun-exposed trunk skin in a patient with longstanding dysplastic nevus syndrome. No significant tobacco signature (SBS4), APOBEC signature (SBS2/SBS13), or homologous recombination deficiency signature (SBS3) detected.

HER2 (ERBB2) Status: No amplification, no activating mutation detected.

========================================================
GERMLINE DISCLAIMER
========================================================

This assay is performed on tumor (somatic) tissue and is NOT a germline genetic test. Variants detected may be somatic, germline, or clonal hematopoietic in origin. Variants with potential germline relevance (e.g., TP53 p.R248W at VAF 41%) may warrant referral to a certified genetic counselor for germline evaluation in the appropriate clinical context, particularly given the patient's personal history of dysplastic nevus syndrome and family history of cutaneous melanoma in a first-degree relative. Germline evaluation is outside the scope of this report.

Note regarding TP53 p.R248W (VAF 41%): The elevated VAF for this variant, in the context of estimated 65% tumor cellularity, is compatible with a somatic heterozygous mutation. However, given the strong family history and personal history, germline TP53 testing may be considered at the discretion of the treating team and genetic counseling.

========================================================
INTERPRETATION AND MOLECULAR PROFILE SUMMARY
========================================================

This comprehensive NGS profile reveals a complex genomic landscape consistent with aggressive Stage IV UV-induced cutaneous melanoma. The dominant molecular subtype is BRAF V600E-driven (MAPK pathway-activated), with co-occurring alterations that elaborate on the tumor's biology and potential therapeutic vulnerabilities:

1. BRAF V600E (VAF 48%) — Confirmed oncogenic driver; directly actionable with FDA-approved BRAF/MEK inhibitor combinations (dabrafenib/trametinib, vemurafenib/cobimetinib, or encorafenib/binimetinib).

2. NF1 p.Q1300* (VAF 52%) — RAS pathway activator via loss of neurofibromin GAP function. Co-occurrence with BRAF V600E is notable; NF1 loss may drive RAS-mediated bypass signaling and contribute to early acquired resistance to BRAF inhibition.

3. CDKN2A/CDKN2B homozygous co-deletion — Complete loss of INK4 locus tumor suppressors (p16, p15, and p14/ARF), eliminating CDK4/6 inhibitory checkpoints and p53 stabilization via ARF-MDM2 axis.

4. High TMB (42 mut/Mb) — Markedly elevated, consistent with UV mutagenesis; favorable biomarker for immune checkpoint inhibitor responsiveness.

5. PD-L1 TPS 30% / CPS 55 (per concurrent IHC) — Consistent with potential immunogenicity of this tumor.

6. PTEN p.L108R (VAF 22%) — Subclonal PI3K/AKT pathway activation; potential resistance mechanism to BRAF-targeted therapy.

7. MAP2K1 copy number gain (5 copies) — Potential intrinsic mechanism of MEK pathway amplification; may reduce efficacy of MEK inhibitor component of BRAF/MEK combination.

8. RAC1 p.P29S (VAF 18%) — UV-signature hotspot, potentially subclonal; associated with primary resistance to BRAF inhibitors through non-MAPK mechanisms.

9. TERT promoter C228T (VAF 55%) — Clonal hallmark mutation of cutaneous melanoma; confirms telomere maintenance as a fundamental feature of this tumor.

10. TP53 p.R248W (VAF 41%) and ARID2 frameshift — Additional tumor suppressor and chromatin remodeling disruption; contribute to overall genomic instability and aggressive phenotype.

OVERALL MOLECULAR INTERPRETATION: This tumor harbors a BRAF V600E-mutant melanoma with multiple co-occurring alterations (NF1 loss, PTEN mutation, MAP2K1 gain, RAC1 p.P29S) that collectively suggest potential for early or acquired resistance to BRAF/MEK-targeted therapy. The markedly elevated TMB and the tumor's PD-L1 expression profile additionally support strong candidacy for immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy, either as an initial strategy or following targeted therapy. Clinical trial enrollment should be considered.

========================================================
FDA-APPROVED MATCHED THERAPIES (THIS TUMOR TYPE)
========================================================

- Dabrafenib (Tafinlar) + Trametinib (Mekinist): FDA-approved for BRAF V600E/K-mutant unresectable or metastatic melanoma [BRAF V600E confirmed]
- Vemurafenib (Zelboraf) + Cobimetinib (Cotellic): FDA-approved for BRAF V600E/K-mutant unresectable or metastatic melanoma [BRAF V600E confirmed]
- Encorafenib (Braftovi) + Binimetinib (Mektovi): FDA-approved for BRAF V600E/K-mutant unresectable or metastatic melanoma [BRAF V600E confirmed]
- Pembrolizumab (Keytruda): FDA-approved for unresectable or metastatic melanoma; also FDA-approved for TMB-High (≥10 mut/Mb) solid tumors [TMB 42 mut/Mb]
- Nivolumab (Opdivo): FDA-approved for unresectable or metastatic melanoma (monotherapy and in combination with ipilimumab)
- Ipilimumab (Yervoy) + Nivolumab (Opdivo): FDA-approved for unresectable or metastatic melanoma
- Ipilimumab (Yervoy) monotherapy: FDA-approved for unresectable or metastatic melanoma
- Nivolumab (Opdivo) + Relatlimab (Opdualag): FDA-approved for unresectable or metastatic melanoma

========================================================
TECHNICAL NOTES AND LIMITATIONS
========================================================

- This assay detects somatic alterations with VAF ≥5% and does not detect alterations below this threshold. Low-level subclonal variants may be missed.
- Homopolymer regions and regions of low complexity may have reduced sensitivity for indel detection.
- Copy number analysis requires adequate tumor purity (≥20%); estimated tumor content of 65% is sufficient.
- This panel does not comprehensively assess all possible alterations in all cancer-relevant genes. Alterations in genes not included on the panel cannot be excluded.
- Variants of uncertain significance (VUS) not meeting reporting thresholds are not included in this report.
- Interpretation is based on current knowledge at the time of reporting; the clinical significance of certain variants may evolve as evidence accumulates.
- Tissue heterogeneity and clonal evolution may result in molecular differences between the specimen analyzed and other sites of metastatic disease.

========================================================
PATHOLOGIST / LABORATORY DIRECTOR ATTESTATION
========================================================

This report was reviewed and authorized by a board-certified molecular pathologist. Results were interpreted in the clinical context provided by the ordering physician. Questions regarding this report should be directed to the Molecular Pathology Laboratory.

Report Status: FINAL

[Electronically signed — Molecular Pathology Laboratory Director, MD, PhD, FCAP]
[CAP/CLIA Laboratory Accreditation — Certificate on file]
